findings.description: Off-by-one: offset = page * page_size with a 1-based page silently skips the first full page, so page 1 returns results 21-40 instead of 1-20.
User-supplied name_pattern is compiled and run as a raw regex with no timeout or complexity guard, enabling catastrophic-backtracking ReDoS (e.g. '(a+)+$').
Pseudo-pagination fetches every matching row into memory and sorts/slices in Python instead of pushing LIMIT/OFFSET/ORDER BY to SQL, so cost grows with the whole table per request.
matcher.search(u['display_name']) assumes display_name is non-null; a NULL value from the DB yields None and raises TypeError, crashing the search.
page_size is only upper-bounded; page_size<=0 yields an empty/garbage window and negative values produce surprising slice behavior with no validation.
page is never validated; page=0 gives offset 0 (wrong vs 1-based contract) and negative page yields a negative offset that slices from the end of the list.
users.sort recomputes display_name.lower() on every comparison; on large result sets a key cache or SQL-side ORDER BY would avoid redundant lowercasing.
sqlite3.connect opens the DB without a timeout or read-only/URI guard, so a locked or oversized DB blocks the request indefinitely with no graceful degradation.

Logging only counts/page numbers is fine, but the surrounding methods surface user PII (email, display_name) with no field-level access control noted; confirm callers are authorized to read the full directory.
Typing imports Optional from typing while using PEP 585 builtins (list[dict]) elsewhere; mixing styles is inconsistent and Optional could be the | None form.
